perturbation.original_text: Athlete and Wade Enterprises, jointly and severally, hereby agree to indemnify, defend and hold harmless Naked, its shareholders, directors, officers, employees, agents, and affiliates (referred to collectively as "Naked Indemnities") from and against any and all claims, actions, causes or action, damages, injuries, expenses, liabilities (joint and several), penalties fines, attorneys' fees, court costs, and any other expenses incurred by Naked Indemnities arising out of or are in any way connected directly or indirectly with any and all claims, suits, actions, costs, and other expenses, fines, judgments, investigations, proceedings, demands, liabilities, and obligations of any nature whatsoever, with respect to Wade Enterprises' or Athlete's breach of its respective representations and warranties, uncured breach of this Agreement or Athlete's gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.changed_text: Athlete and Wade Enterprises, jointly and severally, hereby agree to indemnify Naked Indemnities from and against damages and expenses reasonably connected with Wade Enterprises' or Athlete's uncured breach of this Agreement or Athlete's actions.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text significantly weakens the indemnification clause by limiting it to 'damages and expenses reasonably connected' and removing the extensive list of potential liabilities. The phrase 'Athlete's actions' is too vague. This creates an ambiguity about the scope of indemnification and makes it harder to enforce compared to the original detailed provision.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-322.1 addresses agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence in connection with the construction, maintenance, and repair of real property. While this contract doesn't directly deal with real property, the principle of clearly defining the scope of indemnity is crucial. The modified text’s ambiguous language could be interpreted as an attempt to limit liability without specific details, which goes against the spirit of ensuring accountability, potentially making the indemnity provision unenforceable under similar legal scrutiny regarding clarity and scope. The change introduces ambiguity and reduces the scope of responsibility, potentially conflicting with the intent of the law requiring clear assumption of risk and liability.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-322.1 Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-322.1. Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases. 1. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee against liability for damage arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property contributed to, caused by or resulting from the negligence of the promisee, his agents or employees, or indemnitee, whether such negligence be in whole or in part, is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers' compensation agreement or other agreement issued by an admitted insurer. This subdivision shall not preclude a promisee requiring indemnification for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property caused by or resulting from the negligence of a party other than the promisee, whether or not the promisor is partially negligent. 2. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to condition a subcontractor's or materialman's right to file a claim and/or commence an action on a payment bond on exhaustion of another legal remedy is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this subdivision shall not affect the validity of any insu
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the original indemnification clause by limiting it to 'damages and expenses reasonably connected' and making 'Athlete's actions' too vague. The original text had an extensive list of potential liabilities. This creates an ambiguity about the scope of indemnification and makes it harder to enforce compared to the original detailed provision. The Law Explanation states that this ambiguity could be interpreted as an attempt to limit liability without specific details, which goes against the spirit of ensuring accountability, potentially making the indemnity provision unenforceable. NY General Obligations Law § 5-322.1, as seen in Scraped Law Snippet 2, addresses agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence, indicating a principle of clearly defining the scope of indemnity. The change introduces ambiguity and reduces the scope of responsibility, potentially conflicting with the intent of the law requiring clear assumption of risk and liability, therefore creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Naked shall comply with all applicable rules and regulations (including the NBA's) in its use of the Commercial Materials hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Naked shall endeavor to comply with applicable rules and regulations (including the NBA's) in its use of the Commercial Materials hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing "shall comply" with "shall endeavor to comply", the obligation becomes less strict and more discretionary. "Endeavor to comply" implies an effort to comply but doesn't guarantee it, creating ambiguity about the extent of Naked's responsibility.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act prohibits false or misleading advertising. By weakening the obligation to 'endeavor to comply,' Naked could potentially use commercial materials that are misleading or infringe on the rights of others (including the NBA), leading to violations of the Lanham Act. If Naked only 'endeavors' to comply, it may not take sufficient steps to ensure that its advertising is truthful and non-infringing, potentially leading to false advertising claims or trademark infringement claims under the Lanham Act. The original text requires complete compliance, preventing such legal issues.

False designations of origin, false descriptions, and dilution forbidden (a) Civil action (1) Any person who, on or in connection with any goods or services, or any container for goods, uses in commerce any word, term, name, symbol, or device, or any combination thereof, or any false designation of origi
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates full compliance ("shall comply") with all applicable rules and regulations, including those of the NBA. The modified text weakens this obligation to merely "endeavor to comply," implying an effort to comply without guaranteeing it. This creates ambiguity about Naked's responsibility and opens the door to potential non-compliance. The LLM explanation suggests this shift could lead to the use of misleading commercial materials, potentially infringing on the rights of others and leading to Lanham Act violations related to false advertising or trademark infringement. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 15 USC 1125, addresses "False designations of origin, false descriptions, and dilution forbidden." By only endeavoring to comply, Naked may not take sufficient steps to ensure its advertising is truthful, potentially violating the Lanham Act. The original strict compliance prevented this risk more effectively, thus the modification introduces a legal contradiction based on the 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: All uses of Wade Image in connection with this Agreement shall be subject to the prior written approval of Wade, which Wade shall not unreasonably withhold.
perturbation.changed_text: All uses of Wade Image in connection with this Agreement should ideally be subject to Wade's approval.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "shall be subject to the prior written approval of Wade, which Wade shall not unreasonably withhold" to "All uses of Wade Image in connection with this Agreement should ideally be subject to Wade's approval," the mandatory requirement for prior written approval is weakened. The phrase "should ideally" introduces ambiguity and makes the approval process less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3344 protects an individual's right to control the commercial use of their name, image, and likeness. By making Wade's approval "ideal" rather than mandatory, Naked could potentially use Wade's image without proper consent, violating his right of publicity under California law. The original language provides clear protection through the requirement of prior written approval, while the changed language undermines that protection by implying that approval is not always necessary, contradicting the intent of the statute.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. COMPENSATORY RELIEF [3281 - 3361] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Measure of Damages [[3300.] - 3361] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 3. Penal Damages [3344 - 3346] ( Article 3 enacted 1872. ) 3344. (a) Any person who knowingly uses another’s name, voice, signature, photograph, or likeness, in any manner, on or in products, merchandise, or goods, or for purposes of advertising or selling, or soliciting purchases of, products, merchandise, goods or services, without such person’s prior consent, or, in the case of a minor, the prior consent of his parent or legal guardian, shall be liable for any damages sustained by the person or persons injured as a result thereof. In addition, in any action brought under this section, the person who violated the section shall be liable to the injured party or parties in an amount equal to the greater of seven hundred fifty dollars ($750) or the actual damages suffered by him or her as a result of the unauthorized use, and any profits from the unauthorized use that are attributable to the use and are not taken into account in computing the actua
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the requirement for prior written approval for using Wade's image, potentially violating California Civil Code § 3344, which requires prior consent for using someone's likeness for commercial purposes. The original text stated approval 'shall not unreasonably withhold,' enforcing a clear requirement. The modified text uses 'should ideally,' making approval discretionary and less enforceable. Snippet 1 directly states that 'Any person who knowingly uses another’s… photograph, or likeness… without such person’s prior consent… shall be liable for any damages.' The change allows for potential usage without consent, directly contradicting this statute's intent.
